title: Comparative methods on reticulate phylogenies
text:
This repository contains materials for a workshop at the Botany 2023 conference on 2023-07-23. This session is part of the workshop “Methodological advances in reticulate evolution” organized by George Tiley & Claudia Solís-Lemus.
Goal: learn to analyze trait data using phylogenetic comparative methods (PCMs) on reticulate phylogenies, modelled by phylogenetic networks –also named admixture graphs when modelling closely-related populations. We will use methods implemented PhyloNetworks.
You can find and clone the workshop material at this github repository.

preparation
What you should do prior to the workshop:

install Julia and add it to your PATH
install VSCode or another editor, if you don’t have one already.

If you use VSCode, like we will demonstrate, then within VSCode:

add code to your PATH, by opening the command palette then searching for “shell command”
within VSCode, install the Julia extension and the Quarto extension.

Install Julia and some packages:

Open julia, either in a terminal, or terminal integrated in VSCode, or by opening the app
within julia, install PhyloNetworks and PhyloPlots.

title: Workshop overview
text: Comparative methods are meant to answer questions about trait evolution. In this workshop, we will learn about methods on networks:

title: Workshop overview
text:
topics we will cover

How to calibrate a network, if needed
continuous response: phylogenetic regression & phylogenetic ANOVA when the phylogeny is a network. Predictors can be continuous or discrete.

ancestral state reconstruction
how account for within-species variation in the response trait
how to measure the impact of gene flow

discrete trait

ancestral state reconstruction
was the trait via gene flow?

How to visualize the results

title: Workshop overview
text:
let’s get started
load the packages we will use:


Code
using CSV               # read/write CSV and similar formats
using DataFrames        # versatile tabular data format
using PhyloNetworks     # includes many utilities
using PhyloPlots        # for plotting networks: via R
using RCall             # run R within Julia


We will use data on Polemonium from Rose et al. 2021 and Teo et al. 2023.



Polemonium californicum, by Jeffrey Rose


first load the network topology:


Code
snaq_net = readTopology("data/polemonium_network_fromSNaQ.phy");
R"par"(mar=[0,0,0,0], cex=0.7); # 0 margins, cex<1 for smaller 'character expansion'
plot(snaq_net; showedgelength=true, xlim=[0,18]); # increased limit to see full taxon labels





then the trait data


Code
traits_indiv = CSV.File("data/polemonium_traits_individual.csv") |> DataFrame
